question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
Is she conscious?
Is she breathing normally?
How many weeks pregnant is she?
Is she having contractions? How far apart are they?
Has her water broken? What color was the fluid?
Is there any vaginal bleeding? How much — spotting, or soaking through pads?
Is she in pain? Where is the pain?
Does she feel the urge to push?
Can you see the baby's head or any part of the baby?
Has she had a seizure?
Does she have a severe headache or vision changes?
Is there swelling of the face or hands?
Is she dizzy or lightheaded?
Has she had any complications with this pregnancy?
How many previous pregnancies and deliveries has she had?
Has she had any previous C-sections?
Is this a high-risk pregnancy?
Is the baby moving normally?
Does she have a fever?
Was there any trauma or fall?
ECHO-level: Unconscious, not breathing — maternal cardiac arrest
DELTA-level: Active heavy hemorrhage — soaking through pads continuously
DELTA-level: Eclamptic seizure — seizing during pregnancy
DELTA-level: Umbilical cord prolapse — cord visible before baby
DELTA-level: Imminent delivery with complications — breech, cord around neck
CHARLIE-level: Imminent uncomplicated delivery — baby crowning, contractions < 2 min apart
CHARLIE-level: Severe preeclampsia — severe headache, visual changes, hypertension symptoms
condition: severe headache AND visual changes AND edema

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Premature labor (<37 weeks) with active contractions
BRAVO-level: Moderate vaginal bleeding, stable vital signs, conscious
condition: moderate bleeding AND stable AND conscious

question_text: BRAVO-level: Regular contractions < 5 min apart, term pregnancy, no complications
condition: contractions < 5 min AND term AND no complications

question_text: ALPHA-level: Mild cramping or spotting, term pregnancy, no other symptoms
condition: mild symptoms AND term AND stable

question_text: OMEGA-level: Braxton-Hicks contractions, no bleeding, baby moving normally, follow-up with OB
condition: Braxton-Hicks AND no bleeding AND fetal movement normal

question_text: Have her lie on her LEFT side — this improves blood flow to the baby.
If delivery is imminent: support the baby's head as it emerges. Do NOT pull on the baby.
If the cord is around the baby's neck, gently slip it over the head. Do NOT cut or clamp the cord.
After delivery, keep the baby at the level of the mother. Dry the baby and keep it warm.
If heavy bleeding: apply firm pressure with clean towels to the vaginal area. Elevate the legs.
Do NOT give the patient anything to eat or drink.
If she becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR. If pregnant and > 20 weeks, tilt her slightly to the LEFT during CPR.
Stay on the line. Help is on the way.
If the situation changes — heavier bleeding, baby coming, or she stops breathing — call us back immediately.
